docs.title: PLCC-ng
docs.text:
PLCC-ng is the next generation of the Programming Languages Compiler Compiler (PLCC), an educational tool for teaching and learning programming language concepts.

With PLCC-ng, students can describe a language's syntax using regular expressions and BNF, implement its semantics in Java or Python, and immediately experiment with scanning, parsing, and semantic evaluation using dedicated command-line tools.

docs.title: Where to start
docs.text:
• Quick Start — Install plcc-ng and run your first specification in under 10 minutes.
  • Language Guide — Token rules, BNF syntax, code generation, worked examples.
  • CLI Reference — Every command, every flag.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This page covers installing plcc-ng via pip. Additional installation methods will be documented here as they become available.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.12 or later
  • Java JDK 21 or later (only needed if implementing semantics in Java)

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install plcc-ng

Verify the installation:

plcc-version

docs.title: Upgrade
docs.text:
pip install --upgrade plcc-ng

Verify the new version:

plcc-version

Check the release notes for breaking changes between versions before upgrading.

docs.title: Pin a specific version
docs.text:
To install a specific version:

pip install plcc-ng==X.Y.Z

To pin in requirements.txt:

plcc-ng==X.Y.Z

To pin in pyproject.toml:

[project]
dependencies = [
    "plcc-ng==X.Y.Z",
]

Check which version is currently installed:

plcc-version

docs.title: Uninstall
docs.text: pip uninstall plcc-ng

docs.title: Migration from PLCC to PLCC-ng
docs.text: PLCC-ng is a modern, pip-installable rewrite of PLCC. It adds Python semantics support, a cleaner CLI, and a more extensible architecture. It is not backward compatible — spec files and command invocations both require updates.

docs.title: 1. Install PLCC-ng
docs.text:
Replace the PLCC shell script, Docker, or DevContainer install with:

pip install plcc-ng

See Installation for upgrade, pinning, and uninstall instructions.

docs.title: 2. Rename your grammar file
docs.text:
PLCC's default grammar file is named grammar. PLCC-ng's default is spec.plcc.

grammar  →  spec.plcc

docs.title: 3. Update regex patterns in the lexical section
docs.text:
PLCC uses Java regex (java.util.regex.Pattern). PLCC-ng uses Python regex (re).

Most common patterns are identical. The following Java-specific syntax must be rewritten:

Java pattern Python equivalent \p{Alpha} [a-zA-Z] (?<name>...) named group (?P<name>...) \p{Digit} \d

Test your patterns with a Python regex tool (e.g. regex101.com set to Python flavor).

docs.title: 4. Check scan algorithm behavior
docs.text:
PLCC gives skip rules priority over all token rules whenever they match any input, regardless of match length. PLCC-ng uses pure first-longest-match — skip and token rules compete equally, with ties broken by declaration order.

If any of your skip rules are shorter than competing token rules, the behavior changes:

Input PLCC PLCC-ng 123 with skip ONE '1' before token NUMBER '\d+' skip wins (nothing emitted) NUMBER '123' (longer match wins)

Fix: If you relied on skip-first behavior, rewrite the skip pattern to match as many characters as the token it was shadowing. For most grammars (whitespace skips vs. identifier/number tokens), the longest match already produces the same result and no change is needed.

docs.title: 5. Update nonterminal names to PascalCase
docs.text:
PLCC nonterminals are lowercase; PLCC-ng uses PascalCase.

PLCC PLCC-ng <prog> <Program> <exp> <Expr>

Update every nonterminal on both the left-hand side and right-hand side of every rule.

docs.title: 6. Update alternative/subclass syntax
docs.text:
PLCC places the distinguishing name as a suffix after the closing bracket. PLCC-ng places it after a colon inside the brackets.

PLCC PLCC-ng <exp>WholeExp ::= ... <Expr:WholeExp> ::= ... <exp>SubExp ::= ... <Expr:SubExp> ::= ...

docs.title: 7. Update captured field syntax
docs.text:
PLCC places a field name as a suffix after the closing bracket. PLCC-ng places it after a colon inside the brackets.

PLCC PLCC-ng <exp>exp1 (field named exp1) <Expr:exp1> <exp>exp2 (field named exp2) <Expr:exp2> <WHOLE> (auto-named field whole) <WHOLE> (same — auto-naming unchanged) <WHOLE> with explicit name <WHOLE:whole>

docs.title: 8. Add semantic section language header
docs.text:
PLCC only supports Java semantics and has no language header. PLCC-ng requires the first non-blank line of the semantic section to declare the language.

Before (PLCC): 

%
Prog
%%%
  public void $run() { ... }
%%%

After (PLCC-ng): 

%
Java

Prog
%%%
  public void _run() { ... }
%%%

The supported values are Java and Python.

docs.title: 9. Rename the entry point method
docs.text:
The method the start symbol's class uses as its execution entry point was renamed.

PLCC PLCC-ng $run() _run()

Update this in the semantic section of your spec file.

docs.title: 10. Check %include paths
docs.text: Both PLCC and PLCC-ng support %include FILENAME. The syntax is unchanged. If your include paths were relative to a file named grammar, they will work unchanged — paths are resolved relative to the file that contains the %include directive.

docs.title: 11. Replace commands
docs.text:
PLCC PLCC-ng Notes plcc.py --version plcc-version plccmk [-c] [file] (not needed) Top-level commands generate and compile automatically scan [file...] plcc-scan [file...] Token output format changed (see below) parse [-t] [-n] [file...] plcc-parse [file...] Always shows parse tree; no -t flag needed rep [-t] [-n] [file...] plcc-rep [file...] parse --json_ast plcc-tokens \| plcc-trees See below

Token output format change:

PLCC format: 

   1: WHOLE '3'

PLCC-ng format (file:line:column): 

-:1:1 NUM '42'

JSON parse trees:

PLCC's --json_ast flag (passed to plccmk and parse) is not available in PLCC-ng. To obtain a JSON parse tree, use the lower-level pipeline:

plcc-spec spec.plcc > plcc-ng/spec.json
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-ll1 > plcc-ng/ll1.json
plcc-tokens plcc-ng/spec.json samples/ | plcc-trees --ll1=plcc-ng/ll1.json

See plcc-trees for details.

docs.title: Features not yet in PLCC-ng
docs.text:
• Docker installation — PLCC supported Docker via plcc-con. PLCC-ng is currently pip-only. Docker support is planned.
  • DevContainer — PLCC provided a ready-to-use devcontainer image. PLCC-ng does not yet provide one.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.12 or later
  • Java JDK 21 or later (only necessary if implementing semantics in Java)

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install plcc-ng

For upgrade, version pinning, and uninstall instructions, see Installation.

docs.title: Test drive
docs.text: Let's build a tiny language whose programs consist of a sequence of integers. Running a program will print their sum.

docs.title: 1. Define the language
docs.text:
Create a file named spec.plcc:

PythonJava 
# Define the tokens of the language.
skip  WHITESPACE '\s+'
token NUM '\d+'

%

# Define the structure of the language.
# A program consists of a sequence of numbers.
<Program> **= <NUM:num>

%

# Define what happens when a program is run.
Python

Program
%%%
def _run(self):
  print(sum(int(str(num)) for num in self.numList))
%%%

# Define the tokens of the language.
skip  WHITESPACE '\s+'
token NUM '\d+'

%

# Define the structure of the language.
# A program consists of a sequence of numbers.
<Program> **= <NUM:num>

%

# Define what happens when a program is run.
Java

Program
%%%
public void _run() {
    int sum = 0;
    for (NUM num : numList) {
        sum += Integer.parseInt(num.lexeme);
    }
    System.out.println(sum);
}
%%%

PLCC-ng automatically generates fields such as numList from the grammar. The Language Guide explains how this mapping works.

docs.title: 2. Scan source text
docs.text:
In the same directory as spec.plcc:

echo "42 36 2" | plcc-scan

plcc-scan automatically discovers the specification file.

Output:

-:1:1 NUM '42'
-:1:4 NUM '36'
-:1:7 NUM '2'

docs.title: 3. Parse source text
docs.text:
In the same directory:

echo "42 36 2" | plcc-parse

Output:

Program
  NUM '42' [-:1:1]
  NUM '36' [-:1:4]
  NUM '2' [-:1:7]

docs.title: 4. Run the program
docs.text:
In the same directory:

echo "42 36 2" | plcc-rep

Output:

80

Congratulations! You've defined a language, scanned it with plcc-scan, parsed it with plcc-parse, and executed it with plcc-rep.

docs.title: What next
docs.text:
• Language Guide - Learn to specify languages.
  • CLI Reference - Learn about commands.

docs.title: What's new
docs.text: Curated highlights of what's changed in PLCC-ng and why it matters to you. For the full commit-level history, see GitHub Releases.

docs.title: 2026-07-06 — PLCC-ng v1.0.0
docs.text: PLCC-ng reaches 1.0: the next generation of PLCC is ready for classroom use. If you're coming from PLCC, here's a tour of what's changed and why it matters. Migrating a course? Start with the migration guide.

docs.title: Write semantics in four languages
docs.text: PLCC generated Java, and only Java. PLCC-ng generates scanners, parsers, and interpreters in Python, Java, Haskell, or JavaScript — pick the language your course teaches and write your semantics in it. Start with the Language Guide, then see the per-language pages for Python, Java, Haskell, and JavaScript.

docs.title: Simpler native installation
docs.text: Installing PLCC-ng natively is now a single pip install plcc-ng — no shell-script installer to fetch and configure. See Installation for upgrade, pinning, and uninstall instructions.

docs.title: Three commands to run your language
docs.text: Day-to-day work needs just three commands, with no separate compile step: plcc-scan tokenizes input, plcc-parse shows the parse tree, and plcc-rep runs your full language in a read-eval-print loop. Each one orchestrates smaller composable commands underneath — emitting generated code, building it, feeding it input — that you can run directly to explore each stage of the pipeline and how the pieces fit together. See Author-facing commands and Under the hood.

docs.title: Diagrams from your spec
docs.text: The plcc-diagram add-on package draws diagrams straight from your spec file: class diagrams of the object model your semantics program against, and syntax (EBNF) diagrams of your grammar. See plcc-diagram and plcc-diagram-syntax.

docs.title: Built to extend
docs.text: The pipeline is open: language extensions add new target languages, parser extensions add new parsing algorithms, and diagram extensions add new visualizations — all discovered and dispatched through the same CLI conventions. See the language, parser, and diagram extension guides.

docs.title: A real documentation site
docs.text: You're reading it. The Language Guide covers every section of a spec file with worked examples, and the CLI reference documents every command and flag.

docs.title: Spec syntax has changed
docs.text: PLCC-ng is not backwards compatible with PLCC: spec files need updating. Regular expressions switch from Java to Python flavor, nonterminals become PascalCase, subclass and captured-field syntax change, and more. The migration guide walks through every change step by step, and its features not yet in PLCC-ng section lists what hasn't made the jump, so you know both sides of the trade before switching.

docs.title: CLI reference
docs.text:
plcc-ng provides a set of command-line tools for defining, building, and experimenting with programming languages. Most language authors interact with only a handful of them day-to-day.

  • Guide — start here to understand which commands to use and how they fit together.
  • All Commands — jump straight to the reference for a specific command.

docs.title: plcc-diagram-build
docs.text:
Dispatch to the appropriate diagram build step. Calls plcc-diagram-<fmt>-build for the specified format, converting a diagram source file into a PNG image.

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-build --input=FILE --output=FILE [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --format=FMT Diagram format plugin to use. Default: plantuml. --input=FILE Path to diagram source file. Required. --output=FILE Path to write PNG image. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-diagram-build --format=plantuml --input=plcc-ng/diagram/diagram.puml --output=plcc-ng/diagram/diagram.png

docs.title: plcc-diagram-class-plantuml-emit
docs.text:
Emit a PlantUML class diagram from model JSON. Reads model JSON from stdin and writes PlantUML source to stdout (or to a file if --output is given).

Called by plcc-diagram-emit --type=class --format=plantuml.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-class-plantuml-emit [--output=DIR] [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Write diagram.puml into this directory. Writes to stdout if omitted. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-diagram-class-plantuml-emit > diagram.puml

docs.title: plcc-diagram-class
docs.text:
Generate and display a class diagram from a PLCC spec file. Shows the classes and inheritance relationships derived from the syntactic grammar.

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-class [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file. Remembered across invocations. Defaults to spec.plcc. --format=FMT Diagram format plugin to use. Default: plantuml. -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version and spec path to stderr. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-diagram-class -s subtract.plcc

docs.title: Diagram formats
docs.text: plcc-diagram-class dispatches to diagram extension plugins via --format. Use plcc-diagram-list to see installed formats. See Diagram extensions for details.

docs.title: plcc-diagram-emit
docs.text:
Dispatch model JSON to the appropriate diagram emitter. Reads model JSON from stdin and calls plcc-diagram-<type>-<fmt>-emit for the specified type and format.

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-emit --type=TYPE [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --type=TYPE Diagram type (e.g., class). Required. --format=FMT Diagram format plugin to use. Default: plantuml. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-diagram-emit --type=class --format=plantuml

docs.title: plcc-diagram-list
docs.text:
List installed diagram format plugins. Scans PATH for plcc-diagram-*-*-emit commands and prints each type/format pair, one per line.

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-list [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-diagram-list
# class/plantuml

docs.title: plcc-diagram-run
docs.text:
Dispatch to the appropriate diagram runner. Calls plcc-diagram-<fmt>-run for the specified format. Currently, all built-in runners print the path to the rendered image file to stdout.

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-run --input=FILE [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --format=FMT Diagram format plugin to use. Default: plantuml. --input=FILE Path to PNG image file. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-diagram-run --format=plantuml --input=plcc-ng/diagram/diagram.png

docs.title: plcc-diagram-syntax-plantuml-emit
docs.text:
Emit a PlantUML EBNF diagram from spec JSON. Reads spec JSON from stdin and writes PlantUML source to stdout.

Called by plcc-diagram-emit --type=syntax --format=plantuml.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-syntax-plantuml-emit [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-diagram-syntax-plantuml-emit > diagram.puml

docs.title: plcc-diagram-syntax
docs.text:
Generate and display a syntax grammar (EBNF) diagram from a PLCC spec file. Shows the grammar rules derived from the syntactic section of the spec.

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram-syntax [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file. Remembered across invocations. Defaults to spec.plcc. --format=FMT Diagram format plugin to use. Default: plantuml. -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version and spec path to stderr. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-diagram-syntax -s subtract.plcc

docs.title: Diagram formats
docs.text: plcc-diagram-syntax dispatches to diagram extension plugins via --format. Use plcc-diagram-list to see installed formats. See Diagram extensions for details.

docs.title: plcc-diagram
docs.text: Generate all installed diagram types from a PLCC spec file. Discovers and runs each installed plcc-diagram-{type} command in alphabetical order.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-diagram [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file. Remembered across invocations. Defaults to spec.plcc.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Option Description -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version and spec path to stderr.

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text: Option Description -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-diagram -s subtract.plcc

docs.title: Diagram types
docs.text: plcc-diagram discovers installed diagram types by scanning PATH for plcc-diagram-{type} executables. Use plcc-diagram-list to see installed formats per type. See Diagram extensions for details.

docs.title: plcc-haskell-build
docs.text:
Build a generated Haskell interpreter with cabal. Runs cabal build in the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-build --target=Haskell.

Requires GHC 9.4 or later and cabal 3.0 or later on PATH.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-haskell-build --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory containing generated Haskell files (from plcc-haskell-emit). Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-haskell-build --output=plcc-ng/Haskell

docs.title: plcc-haskell-emit
docs.text:
Emit a Haskell interpreter from model JSON. Reads model JSON from stdin and writes .hs source files and an interpreter.cabal project file to the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-emit --target=Haskell.

Requires GHC 9.4 or later and cabal 3.0 or later on PATH to build and run the emitted interpreter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-haskell-emit --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory to write generated Haskell files into. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-haskell-emit --output=plcc-ng/Haskell

See the Haskell language guide for how to write Haskell semantic sections.

docs.title: plcc-haskell-run
docs.text:
Run a generated Haskell interpreter. Reads parse tree JSON from stdin and passes it to the interpreter via cabal run interpreter in the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-run --target=Haskell.

Requires cabal 3.0 or later on PATH. Run plcc-haskell-build before running.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-haskell-run --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory containing built Haskell files (from plcc-haskell-build). Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-haskell-run --output=plcc-ng/Haskell

docs.title: plcc-java-build
docs.text:
Compile generated Java source files. Runs javac on all .java files in the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-build --target=Java.

Requires Java JDK 21 or later on PATH.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-java-build --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory containing generated Java files (from plcc-java-emit). Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-java-build --output=plcc-ng/Java

docs.title: plcc-java-emit
docs.text:
Emit a Java interpreter from model JSON. Reads model JSON from stdin and writes .java source files and a Main.java entry point to the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-emit --target=Java.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-java-emit --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory to write generated Java files into. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-java-emit --output=plcc-ng/Java

docs.title: plcc-java-run
docs.text:
Run a compiled Java interpreter. Reads parse tree JSON from stdin and passes it to the Main class in the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-run --target=Java.

Requires Java JDK 21 or later on PATH.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-java-run --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory containing compiled Java class files. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-java-run --output=plcc-ng/Java

docs.title: plcc-javascript-emit
docs.text:
Emit a JavaScript interpreter from model JSON. Reads model JSON from stdin and writes .js class files and a main.js entry point to the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-emit --target=javascript.

Requires Node.js 18 or later on PATH to run the emitted interpreter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-javascript-emit --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory to write generated JavaScript files into. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-javascript-emit --output=plcc-ng/javascript

docs.title: plcc-javascript-run
docs.text:
Run a generated JavaScript interpreter. Reads parse tree JSON from stdin and passes it to main.js in the output directory using node.

Called by plcc-lang-run --target=javascript.

Requires Node.js 18 or later on PATH.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-javascript-run --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory containing generated JavaScript files (from plcc-javascript-emit). Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-javascript-run --output=plcc-ng/javascript

docs.title: plcc-lang-build
docs.text: Dispatch to the appropriate language build step. Calls plcc-<lang>-build for the target language. If no build command is found, exits silently with success — not all languages require a build step (Python does not).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-lang-build [-v ...] --target=LANG --output=DIR

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --target=LANG Target language (e.g. Python, Java). Required. --output=DIR Output directory (already populated by plcc-lang-emit). Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-lang-build --target=Java --output=plcc-ng/Java

docs.title: plcc-lang-emit
docs.text: Dispatch to the appropriate language emitter. Reads model JSON from stdin and calls plcc-<lang>-emit for the specified target language.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-lang-emit [-v ...] --target=LANG --output=DIR

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --target=LANG Target language (e.g. Python, Java). Required. --output=DIR Directory to write emitted source files into. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-lang-emit --target=Python --output=plcc-ng/Python

docs.title: Language plugins
docs.text: plcc-lang-emit calls plcc-<lang>-emit for the target language. Use plcc-lang-list to see available plugins. See Language extensions for details.

docs.title: plcc-lang-list
docs.text: List installed language emitter plugins. Scans PATH for plcc-*-emit commands and prints each discovered language name, one per line.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-lang-list [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-lang-list
# java
# python

docs.title: plcc-lang-run
docs.text: Dispatch to the appropriate language runner. Reads parse tree JSON from stdin and calls plcc-<lang>-run for the target language.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-lang-run --target=LANG --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --target=LANG Target language (e.g. Python, Java). Required. --output=DIR Directory containing built artifacts. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-lang-run --target=Python --output=plcc-ng/Python

docs.title: plcc-ll1
docs.text: Perform LL(1) analysis on a grammar spec. Reads spec JSON from stdin and emits LL(1) analysis JSON to stdout, including FIRST sets, FOLLOW sets, PREDICT sets, any conflicts, and any left-recursion cycles.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-ll1 [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). Higher verbosity emits FIRST/FOLLOW/PREDICT sets and conflict details. --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Analyse a spec and print LL(1) JSON
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-ll1

# Save to file for use with plcc-trees
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-ll1 > plcc-ng/ll1.json

docs.title: plcc-make
docs.text:
Build a PLCC project from a spec file. Runs the full pipeline — spec parsing, LL(1) analysis, code model generation, language emit, and language build — stopping at the stage specified by --through.

Called internally by plcc-scan, plcc-parse, plcc-rep, and plcc-diagram. You can also call it directly to pre-build before running other commands.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-make [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file to build from. Remembered across invocations in the same directory. Defaults to spec.plcc on first use. --through=LEVEL Build up to this level: scan, parse, model, or all. Default: all.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Option Description -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version and spec path to stderr.

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text: Option Description -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Build everything
plcc-make -s subtract.plcc

# Rebuild after editing the spec (spec remembered from previous run)
plcc-make

# Build only through the scan stage
plcc-make --through=scan

docs.title: Build levels
docs.text:
--through value Stages run scan plcc-spec parse plcc-spec, plcc-ll1 model plcc-spec, plcc-model all (default) plcc-spec, plcc-ll1, plcc-model, plcc-lang-emit, plcc-lang-build

plcc-make caches its output in plcc-ng/ and skips stages whose inputs haven't changed since the last successful run.

docs.title: plcc-model
docs.text: Transform spec JSON into a language-neutral code model. The model describes the classes, fields, and inheritance relationships that the language emitter uses to generate source code.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-model [-v ...] [options] [SPEC_JSON]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Argument/Option Description SPEC_JSON Path to spec JSON (output of plcc-spec). Use - or omit to read from stdin. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Pipe from plcc-spec
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model

# From a saved spec JSON
plcc-model plcc-ng/spec.json

docs.title: plcc-parse
docs.text: Parse source input and print the parse tree in human-readable format.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-parse [-v ...] [options] [SOURCE ...]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description SOURCE Source files to parse. Reads stdin if none given.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file to build from. Remembered across invocations in the same directory. Defaults to spec.plcc.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Option Description -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version and spec path to stderr.

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text: Option Description -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Parse stdin
echo "42 36 2" | plcc-parse

# Parse files
plcc-parse -s subtract.plcc samples/

docs.title: Interactive mode
docs.text:
When no source files are given and stdin is a terminal, plcc-parse reads input at a >>> prompt. After each line, complete sentences are parsed and printed immediately. If the input so far is a valid prefix that could be extended (e.g., 3 in a grammar that also accepts 3 + 4), parsing is deferred and a ... continuation prompt appears.

  • Press ^D at the >>> prompt (empty buffer) to exit.
  • Press ^D at the ... prompt to force-submit the buffered input and return to >>>.

docs.title: plcc-parser-list
docs.text: List installed parser plugins. Scans PATH for plcc-parser-* commands and prints each discovered parser kind, one per line.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-parser-list [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-parser-list
# table

docs.title: plcc-parser-table
docs.text: Table-driven LL(1) parser. Reads token JSONL from stdin and emits parse tree JSON to stdout. Called by plcc-trees --parser=table (this is the default parser).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-parser-table [-v ...] [options] --ll1=LL1_JSON

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --ll1=LL1_JSON Path to LL(1) analysis JSON (output of plcc-ll1). Required. -t, --trace Emit parse-step records tracing predict, shift, and complete events. --hold-markers Emit a hold marker after a trailing extensible parse (used by interactive mode). -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-ll1 > plcc-ng/ll1.json
plcc-tokens plcc-ng/spec.json samples/ | plcc-parser-table --ll1=plcc-ng/ll1.json

docs.title: plcc-python-emit
docs.text:
Emit a Python interpreter from model JSON. Reads model JSON from stdin and writes .py class files and a main.py entry point to the output directory.

Called by plcc-lang-emit --target=Python.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-python-emit --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory to write generated Python files into. Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-model | plcc-python-emit --output=plcc-ng/Python

docs.title: plcc-python-run
docs.text:
Run a generated Python interpreter. Reads parse tree JSON from stdin and runs main.py in the output directory using the system Python.

Called by plcc-lang-run --target=Python.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-python-run --output=DIR [-v ...] [options]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --output=DIR Directory containing generated Python files (from plcc-python-emit). Required. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: plcc-python-run --output=plcc-ng/Python

docs.title: plcc-rep
docs.text: REPL — read, eval, print loop for a PLCC spec. Scans and parses source input, then evaluates it using the generated semantics.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-rep [-v ...] [options] [SOURCE ...]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description SOURCE Source files to evaluate. Omit (or pass -) to enter interactive mode.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file to build from. Remembered across invocations in the same directory. Defaults to spec.plcc.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Option Description -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version, spec path, and target language to stderr.

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text: Option Description -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Evaluate files and exit
plcc-rep -s subtract.plcc samples/

# Enter interactive mode
plcc-rep -s subtract.plcc

docs.title: Interactive mode
docs.text:
When no source files are given and stdin is a terminal, plcc-rep reads input at a >>> prompt. After each line, complete sentences are evaluated and their results printed. Continuation prompts (...) appear when the input so far is a valid prefix.

  • Press ^D at the >>> prompt (empty buffer) to exit.
  • Press ^D at the ... prompt to force-submit the buffered input and return to >>>.

docs.title: Startup and errors
docs.text:
When plcc-rep starts, it builds your spec and launches an interpreter subprocess. If the interpreter fails to start — for example, because your semantics code has a syntax error that prevents it from loading — you will see:

Specification error: interpreter failed to start.
Fix the errors in your specification and re-run.

During a session, plcc-rep distinguishes between two categories:

Language behavior — the defined language working as designed. plcc-rep reports the outcome and gives you a fresh prompt:

  • The scanner did not recognize the input (lexical error).
  • The parser found input that does not match any rule (syntax error).
  • Your semantics code raised a LanguageError (semantic rejection — e.g., a type error or division by zero your language author chose to handle explicitly).

Specification errors — a flaw in the language specification itself. Your semantics code threw an unexpected exception (not a LanguageError). plcc-rep prints the error type and message, then exits. Fix the problem in your spec and re-run.

See your language's page for how to raise a LanguageError from semantics code.

docs.title: plcc-scan
docs.text: Tokenize source input and print tokens in human-readable format.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-scan [-v ...] [options] [SOURCE ...]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description SOURCE Source files to tokenize. Reads stdin if none given.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description -h, --help Show usage and exit. -s PATH, --spec=PATH Spec file to build from. Remembered across invocations in the same directory. Defaults to spec.plcc.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Option Description -t, --trace Show detailed scanning output including regex candidates and source lines. -b, --banner Print the plcc-ng version and spec path to stderr.

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text: Option Description -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Tokenize stdin
echo "42 36 2" | plcc-scan

# Tokenize files
plcc-scan -s subtract.plcc samples/

# After setting spec once, subsequent calls remember it
plcc-scan -s subtract.plcc
plcc-scan samples/

docs.title: plcc-spec
docs.text: Parse, validate, and print a PLCC grammar file as JSON.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-spec [-v ...] [options] FILE

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Argument/Option Description FILE PLCC grammar file (.plcc). Use - to read from stdin. --no-json Validate only; do not print JSON to stdout. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Parse and print spec as JSON
plcc-spec spec.plcc

# Validate only (no output on success)
plcc-spec --no-json spec.plcc

# Pipe into another command
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-ll1

docs.title: plcc-tokens
docs.text: Tokenize source files given a spec JSON file; emit token JSONL (one JSON record per line, each record representing a token, skip, or error).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-tokens [-v ...] [options] SPEC_JSON [SOURCE ...]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Argument/Option Description SPEC_JSON Path to spec JSON (output of plcc-spec). SOURCE Source files to tokenize. Use - for stdin. Defaults to stdin. -t, --trace Include regex candidates, source lines, and skip records in output. --source-name=LABEL Override the source label used for stdin. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Tokenize stdin using a spec JSON piped from plcc-spec
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-tokens - samples/

# Save spec JSON first, then tokenize
plcc-spec spec.plcc > plcc-ng/spec.json
plcc-tokens plcc-ng/spec.json samples/

docs.title: plcc-trees
docs.text: Dispatch to a parser plugin. Reads token JSONL from stdin and emits parse tree JSON to stdout. The parser plugin is selected with --parser.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-trees [-v ...] [options] --ll1=LL1_JSON

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Option Description --ll1=LL1_JSON Path to LL(1) analysis JSON (output of plcc-ll1). Required. --parser=KIND Parser plugin to use. Default: table (calls plcc-parser-table). -t, --trace Forward trace flag to the parser plugin. -h, --help Show usage and exit. -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more: -v, -vv, -vvv). --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-spec spec.plcc > plcc-ng/spec.json
plcc-spec spec.plcc | plcc-ll1 > plcc-ng/ll1.json
plcc-tokens plcc-ng/spec.json samples/ | plcc-trees --ll1=plcc-ng/ll1.json

docs.title: Parser plugins
docs.text: plcc-trees calls plcc-parser-<kind> for the given --parser value. Use plcc-parser-list to see available plugins. See Parser extensions for details.

docs.title: plcc-version
docs.text: Print the installed plcc-ng version.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: plcc-version

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
plcc-version
# plcc-ng 1.2.3

docs.title: Author-facing commands
docs.text: These are the commands you interact with directly when working with plcc-ng. Everything else runs behind the scenes.

docs.title: Daily drivers
docs.text: Use these to experiment with your language spec at each stage of the pipeline. They all remember the spec file path between invocations — pass -s <path> once and subsequent commands in the same directory pick it up automatically.

docs.title: plcc-scan
docs.text:
Tokenize source input and print each token in human-readable format. Useful for checking that your lexical rules match what you expect.

echo "42 36 2" | plcc-scan
plcc-scan -s subtract.plcc samples/

docs.title: plcc-parse
docs.text:
Parse source input and print the parse tree. Useful for verifying that your grammar accepts the input you intend and rejects what it shouldn't.

echo "42 36 2" | plcc-parse
plcc-parse -s subtract.plcc samples/

plcc-parse also has an interactive mode: when no source files are given and stdin is a terminal, it reads input at a >>> prompt and parses each line as you type.

docs.title: plcc-rep
docs.text:
Read-eval-print loop. Runs your full language spec — scanning, parsing, and executing semantics — and prints the result of each input.

plcc-rep -s subtract.plcc samples/   # evaluate files and exit
plcc-rep -s subtract.plcc            # enter interactive mode

docs.title: Common options
docs.text:
All commands accept these options:

Option Effect -h, --help Show usage and exit -v Increase verbosity (repeat for more detail: -v, -vv, -vvv) --verbose-format=FMT Verbosity output format: text (default) or json

docs.title: plcc-diagram
docs.text:
Generate and display a class diagram from your spec file. Shows the classes and inheritance relationships that plcc-ng derives from your syntactic grammar — useful for understanding the object model you'll program against when writing semantics.

plcc-diagram -s subtract.plcc

Requires the plcc-diagram package. If plcc-diagram is not installed, this command will not be available.

docs.title: plcc-version
docs.text:
Print the installed plcc-ng version.

plcc-version

docs.title: plcc-lang-list
docs.text:
List the language plugins installed on your system. plcc-ng ships with python and java support; this command shows what is available.

plcc-lang-list

docs.title: plcc-parser-list
docs.text:
List the parser plugins installed on your system. plcc-ng ships with the table parser (LL(1) table-driven); this command shows what is available.

plcc-parser-list

docs.title: plcc-diagram-list
docs.text:
List the diagram plugins installed on your system. Each line shows a type/format pair (e.g., class/plantuml).

plcc-diagram-list

Requires the plcc-diagram package.

docs.title: Extension points
docs.text: plcc-ng is built around extension points. The language, parser, and diagram plugins you see above are the built-in implementations, but the system is designed so that new plugins can be installed alongside plcc-ng and discovered automatically. See the Language extensions, Parser extensions, and Diagram extensions guides for details.

docs.title: Diagram extensions
docs.text: Diagram extensions implement the emit, build, and run steps for a specific diagram format. plcc-ng ships with PlantUML support.

docs.title: How diagram extensions plug in
docs.text:
plcc-diagram runs three dispatch commands:

  1. plcc-diagram-emit --format=FMT — calls plcc-diagram-<type>-<fmt>-emit
  2. plcc-diagram-build --format=FMT — calls plcc-diagram-<fmt>-build
  3. plcc-diagram-run --format=FMT — calls plcc-diagram-<fmt>-run

The default format is plantuml. Use plcc-diagram-list to see what is installed.

docs.title: plcc-plantuml-diagram
docs.text:
Generates a PlantUML class diagram. Rendering is done via the public plantuml.com API — no local PlantUML installation required.

Command What it does plcc-diagram-class-plantuml-emit Reads model JSON from stdin; writes a .puml PlantUML source file plcc-diagram-plantuml-build Sends .puml to plantuml.com and writes the returned PNG plcc-diagram-plantuml-run Prints the path to the rendered PNG

docs.title: plcc-syntax-diagram
docs.text:
Generates a PlantUML EBNF diagram showing the syntactic grammar rules from a PLCC spec file. Rendering is done via the public plantuml.com API — no local PlantUML installation required.

Command What it does plcc-diagram-syntax-plantuml-emit Reads spec JSON from stdin; writes a .puml PlantUML EBNF source file plcc-diagram-plantuml-build Sends .puml to plantuml.com and writes the returned PNG plcc-diagram-plantuml-run Prints the path to the rendered PNG

docs.title: Language extensions
docs.text: Language extensions provide the emit, build, and run steps for a specific target language. plcc-ng ships with Python, Java, JavaScript, and Haskell support.

docs.title: How language extensions plug in
docs.text:
When plcc-make (or any command that calls it) builds a spec with a semantic section, it runs three dispatch commands in sequence:

  1. plcc-lang-emit --target=LANG — calls plcc-<lang>-emit
  2. plcc-lang-build --target=LANG — calls plcc-<lang>-build (no-op if not found)
  3. plcc-lang-run --target=LANG — calls plcc-<lang>-run

The LANG value comes from the language declaration in the spec's semantic section. Use plcc-lang-list to see what is installed.

docs.title: plcc-python
docs.text:
Emits a Python interpreter from model JSON, then runs it with the system Python.

Command What it does plcc-python-emit Writes .py class files and a main.py entry point to the output directory plcc-python-run Runs main.py with the system Python interpreter

No build step is required for Python — plcc-lang-build exits silently if plcc-python-build is not found.

docs.title: plcc-java
docs.text:
Emits a Java interpreter from model JSON, compiles it with javac, then runs it with java.

Command What it does plcc-java-emit Writes .java class files and a Main.java entry point to the output directory plcc-java-build Compiles all .java files with javac; requires Java JDK 21+ on PATH plcc-java-run Runs Main with java; requires Java JDK 21+ on PATH

docs.title: plcc-javascript
docs.text:
Emits a JavaScript interpreter from model JSON, then runs it with Node.js. No build step is required.

Command What it does plcc-javascript-emit Writes .js class files and a main.js entry point to the output directory plcc-javascript-run Runs main.js with node; requires Node.js 18+ on PATH

No build step is required for JavaScript — plcc-lang-build exits silently if plcc-javascript-build is not found.

docs.title: plcc-haskell
docs.text:
Emits a Haskell interpreter from model JSON, compiles it with cabal, then runs it with cabal run.

Command What it does plcc-haskell-emit Writes .hs source files and an interpreter.cabal project file to the output directory plcc-haskell-build Compiles with cabal build; requires GHC 9.4+ and cabal 3.0+ on PATH plcc-haskell-run Runs cabal run interpreter; requires cabal 3.0+ on PATH

docs.title: Parser extensions
docs.text: Parser extensions implement the parsing step. plcc-ng ships with a single built-in parser: plcc-parser-table, an LL(1) table-driven parser.

docs.title: How parser extensions plug in
docs.text:
plcc-trees dispatches to a parser plugin via the --parser=KIND flag (default: table). It calls plcc-parser-<kind>, passing the LL(1) analysis JSON and token JSONL on stdin.

Use plcc-parser-list to see what is installed.

Note: there is no dedicated plcc-parser dispatch command — plcc-trees handles dispatch directly.

docs.title: plcc-parser-table
docs.text:
The default LL(1) table-driven parser. Reads token JSONL from stdin and the LL(1) analysis JSON from --ll1, emits parse tree JSON to stdout.

Command What it does plcc-parser-table Table-driven LL(1) parse; emits parse tree JSON or error records

plcc-parser-table also supports --trace, which emits parse-step records interleaved with the parse tree for debugging.

docs.title: Under the hood
docs.text: The commands you use every day — plcc-scan, plcc-parse, plcc-rep, and plcc-diagram — each orchestrate a pipeline of lower-level commands. This page shows how the pieces fit together.

docs.title: Dependency diagram
docs.text:
The diagram below shows which commands call which. An arrow from A to B means A calls B.

docs.title: The core pipeline
docs.text:
plcc-make is the build orchestrator at the centre of the core package. Every author-facing command calls it before doing its own work.

Command Input → Output plcc-make .plcc spec file → build artifacts in plcc-ng/ plcc-spec .plcc file → spec JSON plcc-ll1 spec JSON → LL(1) analysis JSON plcc-tokens spec JSON + source files → token JSONL plcc-trees token JSONL + LL(1) JSON → parse tree JSON (dispatches to parser plugin) plcc-model spec JSON → language-neutral model JSON plcc-lang-emit model JSON → language source files (dispatches to language plugin) plcc-lang-build language source files → compiled output (dispatches to language plugin) plcc-lang-run compiled output + parse tree JSON → evaluation result (dispatches to language plugin)

docs.title: The diagram pipeline
docs.text:
plcc-diagram (in the plcc-diagram package) calls plcc-make to build the model, then runs its own sub-pipeline:

Command Input → Output plcc-diagram-emit model JSON → diagram source (dispatches to diagram plugin) plcc-diagram-build diagram source → PNG image (dispatches to diagram plugin) plcc-diagram-run PNG path → prints path to stdout (dispatches to diagram plugin)

docs.title: Language guide
docs.text:
A plcc-ng specification describes a language's tokens, syntax, and semantics.

PythonJava 
# Lexical section
skip WHITESPACE /\s+/
token NUM /\d+/

%

# Syntactic section
<Exp> ::= <NUM>

%

# Semantic section
Python

Exp
%%%
def _run(self):
    print("Hello")
%%%

# Lexical section
skip WHITESPACE /\s+/
token NUM /\d+/

%

# Syntactic section
<Exp> ::= <NUM>

%

# Semantic section
Java

Exp
%%%
public void _run() {
    System.out.println("Hello");
}
%%%

docs.title: Comments
docs.text: Comments start with # and end at the end of the current line. Comments may appear anywhere except in semantic section code blocks.

docs.title: Section separators
docs.text: Each section is separated by a line with a single %.

docs.title: Includes
docs.text:
Although you can define an entire language in a single file, larger languages are often easier to maintain when split across multiple files using the %include statement.

%include FILE_PATH

%include is valid in any section, but may not appear in a code block within the semantic section. FILE_PATH is relative to the directory containing the file it appears in.

docs.title: Optional sections
docs.text:
Sections build on one another:

LexicalSyntacticSemantictokens/terminalsclass hiearchy

You can start with only a lexical section (no % separator is needed), or add a syntactic section without defining semantics. This makes it easy to develop and test a language incrementally.

docs.title: What next
docs.text:
• Lexical Section — token and skip syntax, scanning algorithm
  • Syntactic Section — BNF rules, parse tree class hierarchy
  • Semantic Section — embedding code, hooks, target language selection
  • Examples — worked examples of increasing complexity

docs.title: Example: subtraction language
docs.text: This end-to-end example implements a small expression language that evaluates subtraction expressions. It walks through all three grammar sections and shows the output of each pipeline stage.

docs.title: Sample programs
docs.text:
Create a file named samples:

3

-(3,2)

-(-(4,1), -(3,2))

docs.title: Grammar file
docs.text:
Create subtract.plcc:

PythonJava 
# Subtraction language
skip WHITESPACE '\s+'
token WHOLE     '\d+'
token MINUS     '\-'
token LP        '\('
token RP        '\)'
token COMMA     ','
%
<Prog>         ::= <Exp:exp>
<Exp:WholeExp> ::= <WHOLE:whole>
<Exp:SubExp>   ::= MINUS LP <Exp:exp1> COMMA <Exp:exp2> RP
%
Python

Prog
%%%
def _run(self):
    print(self.exp.eval())
%%%

WholeExp
%%%
def eval(self):
    return int(self.whole.lexeme)
%%%

SubExp
%%%
def eval(self):
    return self.exp1.eval() - self.exp2.eval()
%%%

# Subtraction language
skip WHITESPACE '\s+'
token WHOLE     '\d+'
token MINUS     '\-'
token LP        '\('
token RP        '\)'
token COMMA     ','
%
<Prog>         ::= <Exp:exp>
<Exp:WholeExp> ::= <WHOLE:whole>
<Exp:SubExp>   ::= MINUS LP <Exp:exp1> COMMA <Exp:exp2> RP
%
Java

Prog
%%%
public void _run() {
    System.out.println(exp.eval());
}
%%%

WholeExp
%%%
public int eval() {
    return Integer.parseInt(whole.lexeme);
}
%%%

SubExp
%%%
public int eval() {
    return exp1.eval() - exp2.eval();
}
%%%
